title: Fix incorrect multipler for limit field
body:
I want to merge this change because we recently introduced the `values` field with input limit. By accident the query cost was added to incorrect place. This PR fixes this. 

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> The changes on `SelectedAttribute` is expected. We don't have `limit` there, and it is not paginated.  (The query cost for this field will be handled separatly)

# Pull Request Checklist

<!-- Please keep this section. It helps maintainers. -->

- [ ] Privileged queries and mutations are either absent or guarded by proper permission checks
- [ ] Database queries are optimized and the number of queries is constant
- [ ] Database migrations are either absent or optimized for zero downtime
- [ ] The changes are covered by test cases
- [ ] All new fields/inputs/mutations have proper labels added (`ADDED_IN_X`, `PREVIEW_FEATURE`, etc.)
- [ ] All migrations have proper dependencies
- [ ] All indexes are added concurrently in migrations
- [ ] All RunSql and RunPython migrations have revert option defined

step_plan.summary: The PR fixes an incorrect multiplier applied to the limit field by targeting the exact cost-sensitive paths (account filters and SelectedAttributes data loading). It introduces targeted Python-level changes in limit handling, batch-loading optimizations for attributes, and validation for attribute mutations to prevent inflated query costs while preserving correctness.

steps.operation: Review and fix limit multiplier application in filter_number_of_orders
steps.reason: Limit-like behavior is implemented via annotation and range; ensure multiplier targets the intended field and does not inflate query costs when combined with other filters, aligning with the PR note about SelectedAttribute changes.
steps.side_effects: Regression risk in existing number_of_orders queries; add regression tests for limit behavior; potential need for query cost monitoring adjustments.

steps.operation: Review and adjust limit/cost handling in filter_placed_orders
steps.reason: Costly subquery path; validate multiplier usage to avoid duplicative or misplaced costs and consider indexing hints to keep query cost predictable.
steps.side_effects: Tests for edge-case placed_orders filtering; possible index recommendations and minor query plan changes.

steps.operation: Optimize batch loading for all-selected attributes per product
steps.reason: SelectedAttributes loading path is central to query cost; ensure efficient batching, reduce N+1 patterns, and apply correct limit semantics without inflating counts.
steps.side_effects: Potential data shape adjustments and performance improvements; add tests for bulk product attribute loading.

steps.operation: Optimize batch loading of storefront-visible attributes per product
steps.reason: Storefront visibility path must be efficient and correct; ensure batch loading respects visibility rules without unnecessary counting or expensive subqueries.
steps.side_effects: Tests for storefront attribute visibility; potential minor changes to data returned for storefront queries.

steps.operation: Optimize batch loading of selected attributes by product variant
steps.reason: Variant-level attribute loading is a critical hot path; improve batching to minimize DB hits and ensure the final payload matches expectations without extra counting overhead.
steps.side_effects: Performance improvements; add tests around variant-level attribute loading and verify data structure compatibility.

steps.operation: Validate and adjust ProductAttributeAssign mutation for values payload
steps.reason: If the patch touches how values are passed or limited in mutation payloads, enforce input validation and transactional safety to prevent costly or incorrect reordering/assignment operations.
steps.side_effects: Mutation API behavior changes if validation or ordering logic is adjusted; add tests for concurrent reordering scenarios and ensure backward compatibility where appropriate.
